subtitle: Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions
intro: Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from classifying a linear equilibrium by trace and determinant, through building the Jacobian of a nonlinear system, finding fixed points by nullclines, and linearizing the pendulum, up to a full Lotka--Volterra predator--prey analysis with the Jacobian evaluated and the eigenvalues classified at every equilibrium.

takeaway.title: Unit Key Takeaway
takeaway.body: One geometric idea runs through the unit: \highlight{classify the flow one equilibrium at a time}. For a linear system the \highlight{trace and determinant of $A$} name the origin outright. For a nonlinear system, locate the fixed points as \highlight{nullcline intersections}, then linearize with the \highlight{Jacobian $J=\left(\begin{smallmatrix} f_x & f_y \\ g_x & g_y \end{smallmatrix}\right)$} --- the best linear approximation --- and classify each fixed point by its eigenvalues. Stitching those local portraits together along the flow arrows reconstructs the global dynamics, from the swinging pendulum to the cycling predator--prey populations.

problems.problem: \strong{(Classify a linear system)} For $A=\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 2 \\ 0 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$, compute $\tau$ and $\Delta$ and classify the origin of $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$.
problems.solution:
Trace and determinant give the type directly:
\[ \work{\tau=\operatorname{tr}A=-5,\quad \Delta=\det A=(-1)(-4)-0=4>0,\quad \tau^2-4\Delta=25-16=9>0.} \]
With $\Delta>0$, discriminant $>0$, and $\tau<0$, the origin is a \strong{stable node} (eigenvalues $-1,-4$).

problems.problem: \strong{(Spiral or node?)} For $A=\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, find the eigenvalues and classify the origin.
problems.solution:
Here $\tau=0$ and $\Delta=0\cdot 0-(-2)(2)=4>0$:
\[ \work{\det(A-\lambda I)=\lambda^2+4=0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda=\pm 2i.} \]
Pure imaginary eigenvalues with $\tau=0$ make the origin a \strong{center} --- closed orbits around the origin.

problems.problem: \strong{(Eigendirections)} For $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors and state the equilibrium type.
problems.solution:
Expand the characteristic polynomial and solve:
\[ \work{\det(A-\lambda I)=(1-\lambda)^2-4=\lambda^2-2\lambda-3=(\lambda-3)(\lambda+1) \Rightarrow \lambda_1=3,\ \lambda_2=-1.} \]
\[ \work{\lambda_1=3:\ \mathbf{v}_1=(1,2); \qquad \lambda_2=-1:\ \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-2).} \]
Opposite signs $\Rightarrow$ a \strong{saddle}: out along $(1,2)$, in along $(1,-2)$.

problems.problem: \strong{(Build the Jacobian)} For the system $x'=x-xy,\ y'=-y+xy$, write the Jacobian matrix $J(x,y)$ symbolically.
problems.solution:
Here $f=x-xy$ and $g=-y+xy$, so the four partials are
\[ \work{J(x,y)=\begin{pmatrix} f_x & f_y \\ g_x & g_y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1-y & -x \\ y & -1+x \end{pmatrix}.} \]

problems.problem: \strong{(Fixed points by nullclines)} Find all fixed points of $x'=x(3-x-2y),\ y'=y(2-x-y)$.
problems.solution:
Factor each velocity. The $x$-nullclines are $x=0$ or $3-x-2y=0$; the $y$-nullclines are $y=0$ or $2-x-y=0$. Pairing branches:
\[ \work{(0,0),\quad (0,2),\quad (3,0),\quad \text{and } 3-x-2y=0,\ 2-x-y=0 \Rightarrow (1,1).} \]
Four fixed points: $(0,0),(0,2),(3,0),(1,1)$.

problems.problem: \strong{(Linearize the pendulum)} For $x'=y,\ y'=-\sin x$, compute $J(x,y)$ and evaluate it at the fixed points $(0,0)$ and $(\pi,0)$.
problems.solution:
With $f=y,\ g=-\sin x$:
\[ \work{J(x,y)=\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\cos x & 0 \end{pmatrix}; \quad J(0,0)=\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix},\quad J(\pi,0)=\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
(Using $\cos 0=1$ and $\cos\pi=-1$.)

problems.problem: \strong{(Classify the pendulum equilibria)} Using the matrices from Problem 6, find the eigenvalues at $(0,0)$ and $(\pi,0)$ and classify each.
problems.solution:
Take the characteristic polynomial of each:
\[ \work{(0,0):\ \lambda^2+1=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=\pm i \ \text{(center)}; \qquad (\pi,0):\ \lambda^2-1=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=\pm 1 \ \text{(saddle)}.} \]
The hanging-down equilibrium oscillates (center); the inverted equilibrium is an unstable saddle.

problems.problem: \strong{(Predator--prey fixed points)} For $x'=x(2-y),\ y'=y(-3+x)$, find both equilibria.
problems.solution:
Factor: $x(2-y)=0 \Rightarrow x=0$ or $y=2$; $\ y(-3+x)=0 \Rightarrow y=0$ or $x=3$. Pairing the branches,
\[ \work{(0,0)\ \text{(extinction)} \qquad \text{and} \qquad (3,2)\ \text{(coexistence)}.} \]

problems.problem: \strong{(Predator--prey Jacobian)} For the system in Problem 8, evaluate the Jacobian at the coexistence equilibrium and classify it.
problems.solution:
Here $f=2x-xy,\ g=-3y+xy$, so $J=\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 2-y & -x \\ y & -3+x \end{smallmatrix}\right)$. At the coexistence point $(3,2)$,
\[ \work{J(3,2)=\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix},\quad \det(J-\lambda I)=\lambda^2+6=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=\pm i\sqrt{6}.} \]
Pure imaginary eigenvalues $\Rightarrow$ a \strong{center}: predator and prey populations cycle around $(3,2)$.

problems.problem: \strong{(Nonlinear capstone)} For $x'=y,\ y'=-x+x^3$, find all fixed points, evaluate the Jacobian at each, classify them, and describe the global phase portrait.
problems.solution:
Fixed points: $y=0$ and $-x+x^3=x(x^2-1)=0 \Rightarrow (0,0),(\pm 1,0)$. The Jacobian is $J=\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1+3x^2 & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right)$:
\[ \work{J(0,0)=\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow \lambda=\pm i \ \text{(center)}; \quad J(\pm 1,0)=\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow \lambda=\pm\sqrt{2} \ \text{(saddles)}.} \]
Globally: closed orbits around the origin, hemmed in by the separatrices joining the two saddles at $(\pm 1,0)$ --- a conservative double-well picture.
